metadata.title: The Tragedy of Romeo and Juliet
metadata.author: William Shakespeare
metadata.description: Shakespeare's timeless tale of star-crossed lovers from feuding families in Verona. This tragic romance follows Romeo Montague and Juliet Capulet as their forbidden love leads them to secret marriage, family violence, and ultimately their own deaths, which finally reconciles their warring houses.

characters.name: Romeo Montague
characters.description: Son to Montague, the tragic hero

characters.name: Juliet Capulet
characters.description: Daughter to Capulet, the tragic heroine

characters.name: Friar Lawrence
characters.description: A Franciscan friar who marries the lovers

characters.name: Mercutio
characters.description: Romeo's friend, kinsman to Prince Escalus

characters.name: Benvolio
characters.description: Romeo's cousin and friend, a peacekeeper

characters.name: Paris
characters.description: A young nobleman, suitor to Juliet

famous_quotes.quote: But soft, what light through yonder window breaks?
Romeo, Romeo, wherefore art thou Romeo?
What's in a name? That which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet
A plague o' both your houses!
Good night, good night! Parting is such sweet sorrow

title_page.content:
THE TRAGEDY OF ROMEO AND JULIET

by William Shakespeare

[Decorative ornament]

The most excellent and lamentable tragedy of Romeo and Juliet
As it hath been sundry times publicly acted
by the right honourable the Lord Chamberlain's servants

Public Domain Edition

dramatis_personae.title: Dramatis Personæ
dramatis_personae.content:
ESCALUS, Prince of Verona.
MERCUTIO, kinsman to the Prince, and friend to Romeo.
PARIS, a young Nobleman, kinsman to the Prince.
Page to Paris.

MONTAGUE, head of a Veronese family at feud with the Capulets.
LADY MONTAGUE, wife to Montague.
ROMEO, son to Montague.
BENVOLIO, nephew to Montague, and friend to Romeo.
ABRAM, servant to Montague.
BALTHASAR, servant to Romeo.

CAPULET, head of a Veronese family at feud with the Montagues.
LADY CAPULET, wife to Capulet.
JULIET, daughter to Capulet.
TYBALT, nephew to Lady Capulet.
CAPULET'S COUSIN, an old man.
NURSE to Juliet.
PETER, servant to Juliet's Nurse.
SAMPSON, servant to Capulet.
GREGORY, servant to Capulet.
Servants.

FRIAR LAWRENCE, a Franciscan.
FRIAR JOHN, of the same Order.
An Apothecary.
CHORUS.
Three Musicians.
An Officer.
Citizens of Verona; several Men and Women, relations to both houses; Maskers, Guards, Watchmen and Attendants.

setting.title: Setting & Context
setting.content:
SCENE: During the greater part of the Play in Verona; once, in the Fifth Act, at Mantua.

Verona, a city in northern Italy, serves as the backdrop for this tale of feuding families and star-crossed lovers. The action moves between public squares, the houses of the Montagues and Capulets, Friar Lawrence's cell, and finally to the tomb where the lovers meet their fate.

The play unfolds over the course of five days, from Sunday when Romeo and Juliet meet, to Thursday morning when they are found dead together.

prologue.title: THE PROLOGUE
prologue.content:
CHORUS.
Two households, both alike in dignity,
In fair Verona, where we lay our scene,
From ancient grudge break to new mutiny,
Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean.
From forth the fatal loins of these two foes
A pair of star-cross'd lovers take their life;
Whose misadventur'd piteous overthrows
Doth with their death bury their parents' strife.
The fearful passage of their death-mark'd love,
And the continuance of their parents' rage,
Which, but their children's end, nought could remove,
Is now the two hours' traffic of our stage;
The which, if you with patient ears attend,
What here shall miss, our toil shall strive to mend.

[Exit.]

1.title: SCENE I. A public place.
1.stage_directions: Enter Sampson and Gregory armed with swords and bucklers.
1.content:
SAMPSON.
Gregory, on my word, we'll not carry coals.

GREGORY.
No, for then we should be colliers.

SAMPSON.
I mean, if we be in choler, we'll draw.

GREGORY.
Ay, while you live, draw your neck out o' the collar.

SAMPSON.
I strike quickly, being moved.

GREGORY.
But thou art not quickly moved to strike.

SAMPSON.
A dog of the house of Montague moves me.

GREGORY.
To move is to stir; and to be valiant is to stand:
therefore, if thou art moved, thou runn'st away.

[Content continues with the full fight scene, Prince's intervention, Romeo's introduction, and his lovesick state over Rosaline...]

2.title: SCENE II. A Street.
2.content:
CAPULET.
But Montague is bound as well as I,
In penalty alike; and 'tis not hard, I think,
For men so old as we to keep the peace.

[Scene continues with Paris asking for Juliet's hand, Capulet's party invitation, and Romeo learning about Rosaline's attendance...]

0.title: CHORUS
0.content:
Enter Chorus.

CHORUS.
Now old desire doth in his deathbed lie,
And young affection gapes to be his heir;
That fair for which love groan'd for and would die,
With tender Juliet match'd, is now not fair.
[Chorus continues...]

2.title: SCENE II. Capulet's Garden.
2.setting: Capulet's orchard garden, beneath Juliet's window.
2.stage_directions: Enter Romeo. Juliet appears above at a window.
2.content:
ROMEO.
He jests at scars that never felt a wound.

But soft, what light through yonder window breaks?
It is the east, and Juliet is the sun!
Arise fair sun and kill the envious moon,
Who is already sick and pale with grief,
That thou her maid art far more fair than she.

[The famous balcony scene continues with their declarations of love, plans for marriage, and reluctant parting...]

1.title: SCENE I. A public Place.
1.stage_directions: Enter Mercutio, Benvolio, Page and Servants.
1.content:
BENVOLIO.
I pray thee, good Mercutio, let's retire:
The day is hot, the Capulets abroad,
And if we meet, we shall not scape a brawl,
For now these hot days, is the mad blood stirring.

[Scene continues with Tybalt's challenge, Mercutio's death, Romeo's revenge killing of Tybalt, and Romeo's banishment...]

3.title: SCENE III. Juliet's Chamber.
3.setting: Juliet's bedchamber in the Capulet house.
3.content:
JULIET.
Ay, those attires are best; but, gentle Nurse,
I pray thee leave me to myself tonight;
For I have need of many orisons
To move the heavens to smile upon my state,
Which well thou knowest is cross and full of sin.

[Scene continues with Juliet's fears about the potion and her brave decision to drink it...]

3.title: SCENE III. A churchyard; in it a Monument belonging to the Capulets.
3.setting: The Capulet tomb in a churchyard.
3.stage_directions: Enter Paris and his Page with flowers and a torch.
3.content:
PARIS.
Give me thy torch, boy. Hence and stand aloof.
Yet put it out, for I would not be seen.
Under yond yew tree lay thee all along,
Holding thy ear close to the hollow ground.

[The final tragic scene where Romeo finds Juliet seemingly dead, takes poison, Juliet awakens to find Romeo dead, and takes her own life with his dagger...]
